questions.question: Define process scheduling and explain its importance in operating systems.
keyPoints: Process scheduling determines CPU allocation among competing processes
Enables multiprogramming and efficient CPU utilization
Allows multiple processes to make progress
Critical for system responsiveness and throughput

questions.question: Explain the difference between long-term, short-term, and medium-term schedulers.
keyPoints: Long-term controls degree of multiprogramming, selects from job pool
Short-term (CPU scheduler) selects from ready queue to CPU, invoked frequently
Medium-term handles swapping - removes processes from memory temporarily
Each operates at different time scales and frequencies

questions.question: What is context switching? Describe the steps involved in context switching.
keyPoints: Context switching saves state of currently running process
Loads state of new process selected by scheduler
Includes saving/restore of CPU registers, program counter
Also saves/loads memory management information
Involves dispatcher functionality

questions.question: Explain FCFS scheduling algorithm with an example. Also mention its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: FCFS serves processes in order of arrival
Non-preemptive - once CPU assigned, runs to completion
Simple to implement using FIFO queue
Disadvantage: convoy effect, poor average waiting time
Suitable for batch systems only

questions.question: What is Round Robin scheduling? Explain with an example. What is the significance of time quantum?
keyPoints: Preemptive scheduling with fixed time quantum
Processes execute for quantum duration then preempted
Moved to back of ready queue if not complete
Time quantum too large: behaves like FCFS
Time quantum too small: excessive context switching
Optimal quantum balances efficiency and responsiveness

questions.question: Define the five scheduling criteria used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms.
keyPoints: CPU Utilization: percentage of time CPU is busy
Throughput: number of processes completed per unit time
Turnaround Time: time from process submission to completion
Waiting Time: total time process spends in ready queue
Response Time: time from arrival to first CPU allocation

questions.question: Explain Priority Scheduling and discuss the problem of starvation. How can aging help solve starvation?
keyPoints: Priority scheduling assigns priority numbers to processes
Higher priority processes get CPU first
Starvation: low-priority processes may wait indefinitely
Aging gradually increases priority of waiting processes
With aging, old waiting processes eventually get high priority

questions.question: Consider the following processes with their burst times: P1=7, P2=3, P3=8, P4=4. Calculate average waiting time and turnaround time for FCFS and SJF (non-preemptive) scheduling algorithms. Which algorithm performs better and why?
keyPoints: FCFS order: P1,P2,P3,P4 - calculate waiting times: P1=0, P2=7, P3=10, P4=18
Average waiting time FCFS = (0+7+10+18)/4 = 8.75
SJF order by burst: P2(3), P4(4), P1(7), P3(8) - waiting: P2=0, P4=3, P1=7, P3=15
Average waiting time SJF = (0+3+7+15)/4 = 6.25
Turnaround time = waiting + burst for each
SJF performs better with lower average waiting time
SJF is optimal for minimizing waiting time but requires knowledge of burst times

questions.question: Explain Shortest Job First (SJF) and Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) scheduling algorithms. With an example showing arrival times, demonstrate how SRTF preempts and schedules processes.
keyPoints: SJF selects process with minimum burst time, non-preemptive
SRTF is preemptive version, preempts when shorter process arrives
Example with arrivals: P1 at t=0 with burst 8, P2 at t=1 with burst 4
At t=0: P1 runs, at t=1: P2 arrives, P1(7 remaining) vs P2(4), P2 preempts
Continue demonstration showing context switches
SRTF provides better response time but more overhead
Both require knowledge or estimation of burst times

questions.question: What is Multilevel Queue Scheduling? Explain its structure and how it handles different types of processes. Also discuss the limitations and how Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling overcomes them.
keyPoints: MLQ has separate queues for different process types (system, interactive, batch)
Each queue has its own scheduling algorithm
Fixed priority between queues (system > interactive > batch)
Limitation: processes cannot move between queues, may cause starvation
MLFQ allows processes to move between queues
CPU-bound processes demoted, I/O-bound processes promoted
Solves starvation and provides better overall performance

questions.question: Explain the concept of dispatcher in operating systems. What is dispatch latency? How does it affect system performance? Also describe the steps performed by dispatcher.
keyPoints: Dispatcher gives CPU control to process selected by short-term scheduler
Dispatch latency = time to stop one process and start another
Includes context switching time and switching to user mode
Steps: save context of old process, load context of new, jump to PC
Lower dispatch latency improves system responsiveness
Affected by hardware support, number of registers, TLB handling
